Unit 5: The Accusative Case & Basic Prepositions

In Unit 2, you learned that when a noun is the Object (receiving the action), it drops its final -ς (if masculine) and its article becomes τον, την, or το.

Now, we will see the Accusative Case in action through verbs and the "Big Three" prepositions.

1. Verbs that use the Accusative

Most "action" verbs require the object to be in the Accusative case. - Βλέπω τον Γιώργο. (I see George). - Έχω την τσάντα. (I have the bag). - Διαβάζω το βιβλίο. (I read the book).

[!CAUTION] Remember: The verb Είμαι (I am) is special. It acts as a mirror, so it keeps everything in the Nominative (ο, η, το).
- Είμαι ο Γιώργος. (I am George).

2. The Big Three Prepositions (σε, από, με)

In Greek, almost all prepositions force the noun that follows them into the Accusative case.

A. Σε (To / At / In)

This word shows location or direction. It almost always merges with the definite article: - σε + τον = στον - σε + την = στην - σε + το = στο

Example: Πάω στο σινεμά. (I am going to the cinema).

B. Από (From)

Used for origins. It does not merge with the article, but the article must still be in the Accusative. - Είμαι από την Ελλάδα. (I am from Greece). - Έρχομαι από τον γιατρό. (I am coming from the doctor).

C. Με (With)

Used for company or instruments. - Είμαι με τη Μαρία. (I am with Maria). - Θέλω καφέ με ζάχαρη. (I want coffee with sugar).

3. Pronunciation: The Optional 'N'

You will notice the final -ν in words like τον, την, δεν, and μην. In casual speech, Greeks often drop this -ν unless the next word starts with a vowel or a "hard" consonant (κ, π, τ, ξ, ψ). - Τη δασκάλα (The teacher - soft 'δ'). - Την πόρτα (The door - hard 'π').

🎯 Unit 5 Mastery Checklist

  • [ ] I can use βλέπω, έχω, and θέλω with the correct Accusative articles.
  • [ ] I know that σε + την = στην.
  • [ ] I remember to drop the -ς for masculine nouns after από and με.

Up Next: Unit 6: Adjectives & Agreement
